coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanosheet facilitated charge separation and transfer between molybdenum carbide and graphitic carbon nitride toward efficient photocatalytic H2 production
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Yajun
Ma, Dandan
Sun, Diankun
Mao, Siman
He, Chi
Wang, Zeyan
Ji, Xin
Shi, Jian-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Interfacial manipulation of nanostructured heterojunction photocatalysts to enhance charge separation and transfer is highly desirable to achieve a high photocatalytic activity. In this work, a well-designed non-noble-metal Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 heterostructure is constructed, in which the intercalated carbon nanosheets serve as a binder to form an excellent interfacial contact between Mo2C and g-C3N4. In addition, large quantities of carbon quantum dotsare found to be homogeneously embedded in the carbon nanosheets. The as-obtained Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 hybrid exhibits a remarkably improved photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 52.1 μmol h−1 under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) without co-catalyst, which is up to nearly 260 times higher than that of pristine g-C3N4 (0.2 μmol h−1) under the same conditions. The significant increase in photocatalytic activity mainly results from the fast charge migration and separation between Mo2C and g-C3N4 facilitated by the conducting carbon nanosheets as an efficient electron mediator. Moreover, the carbon quantum dots embedded in the carbon support also promotes solar energy utilization. This work highlights a feasible strategy to explore highly efficient photocatalysts via interfacial engineering on heterojunction composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
H2 evolution
Water splitting
Carbon nitride
Heterojunction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism in the Mo2C@C/CN hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A and B) TEM and (C) HRTEM images of Mo2C@C; (D) enlarged view of the circled areas in (C).

TEM images of BCN (A), CN (B) and 1% Mo2C@C/CN (C and D); HRTEM image of Mo2C@C/CN (E); (F) enlarged view of the circled areas in (E); elemental mapping of (G) with the spatially resolved elements of C (H), N (I), Mo (J) and O (K).

(A) XPS survey spectrum of 1% Mo2C@C/CN; (B) Mo 3d XPS spectra of Mo2C@C (a) and 1% Mo2C@C/CN (b); (C) C 1s XPS spectrum of Mo2C@C; (D) O 1s XPS spectrum of Mo2C@C; (E) C 1s XPS spectra of CN (a) and 1% Mo2C@C/CN (b); (F) N 1s XPS spectra of CN (a) and 1% Mo2C@C/CN (b).
UV–vis absorption spectra of CN, Mo2C, Mo2C@C, 1% Mo2C/CN and Mo2C@C/CN.

(A) The average photocatalytic H2 production rates over CN (a), 0.5% Mo2C@C/CN (b), 1% Mo2C@C/CN (c), 2% Mo2C@C/CN (d), 5% Mo2C@C/CN (e), 1% Mo2C/CN (f), Mo2C (g) and Mo2C@C (h) with 80 mg of catalysts under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm); (B) Cyclic runs for the photocatalytic H2 production over 1% Mo2C@C/CN sample with 80 mg of catalyst under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm).
PL spectra (A), transient photocurrent response (B) and EIS Nyquist plots (C) of samples.
Transient fluorescence decay spectra for CN, 1% Mo2C/CN and 1% Mo2C@C/CN. Inset is the table presenting the fluorescence decay lifetimes and their percentages of photo-induced carriers.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure of Mo2C@C/CN.

Schematic illustration of charge transfer processes over Mo2C/CN (A) and Mo2C@C/CN (B); (C) Proposed photocatalytic mechanism in Mo2C@C/CN under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) and the band positions together with H+/H2 and
OH/H2O redox potentials.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanosheet facilitated charge separation and transfer between molybdenum carbide and graphitic carbon nitride toward efficient photocatalytic H2 production

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Center of Nanomaterials for Renewable Energy, School of Electrical Engineering, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment
Center of Nanomaterials for Renewable Energy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Interfacial manipulation of nanostructured heterojunction photocatalysts to enhance charge separation and transfer is highly desirable to achieve a high photocatalytic activity. In this work, a well-designed non-noble-metal Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 heterostructure is constructed, in which the intercalated carbon nanosheets serve as a binder to form an excellent interfacial contact between Mo2C and g-C3N4. In addition, large quantities of carbon quantum dotsare found to be homogeneously embedded in the carbon nanosheets. The as-obtained Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 hybrid exhibits a remarkably improved photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 52.1 μmol h−1 under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm) without co-catalyst, which is up to nearly 260 times higher than that of pristine g-C3N4 (0.2 μmol h−1) under the same conditions. The significant increase in photocatalytic activity mainly results from the fast charge migration and separation between Mo2C and g-C3N4 facilitated by the conducting carbon nanosheets as an efficient electron mediator. Moreover, the carbon quantum dots embedded in the carbon support also promotes solar energy utilization. This work highlights a feasible strategy to explore highly efficient photocatalysts via interfacial engineering on heterojunction composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is considered as an ideal energy carrier in the future due to its high mass-specific energy density and zero emission characteristics [1]. Among various hydrogen production processes, photocatalytic water splitting has emerged as an efficient and eco-friendly approach to produce hydrogen energy. Until now, a large number of semiconductor materials including metal oxides, sulfides and nitrides have been exploited as photocatalysts [2–13]. Recently, the design of visible-light-active photocatalysts has been vastly pursued by researchers for effective utilization of the solar spectrum, which consists of a large proportion of visible light (ca. 43%) [14–16]. The large band gap energy of semiconductors, such as the traditional TiO2 photocatalyst, severely restricts their visible-light applications [17–19]. Therefore, in the search of robust and visible-light-responsive photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has gained considerable attention as one of the most promising semiconductor materials for visible-light-induced H2 generation owing to its appropriate medium band gap (ca. 2.7 eV), high chemical and thermal stability, as well as pollution-free feature [20–23]. Importantly, g-C3N4 can be easily fabricated by thermal polymerization of abundant nitrogen-rich precursors such as melamine, dicyandiamide, cyanamide, and urea [24–27]. Moreover, due to the polymeric feature of g-C3N4, its surface chemistry can be easily modulated at molecular level [28,29]. Thus, with all the outstanding properties possessed by the g-C3N4, it has been regarded as a promising metal-free semiconductor photocatalyst. However, pristine g-C3N4 exhibits unsatisfactory photocatalytic efficiency due to its insufficient solar light absorption, low surface area and the fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [30].

During photocatalytic reactions, photogenerated charge carriers are mainly involved in three processes, which include charge generation, charge separation and transfer, as well as surface reactions [31–33]. Therefore, delicate manipulation of photogenerated charge carriers in semiconductor photocatalysts is essential for enhancing the activities of semiconductor photocatalysts. In particular, acceleration of the transport process and suppression of recombination have been proved to hold the key to efficient solar-to-chemical energy conversion, which can be effectively achieved by constructing heterojunctions [34–40]. Moreover, to facilitate the charge transfer between the two semiconductors in heterojunctions, several metals, such as Au, Ag and Cu, have been reported to be employed as electron mediator [41–43]. However, embedding such metal particles into the interfaces of two semiconductors could only result in a limited point-contact between the metal particles and the two semiconductors, which inevitably leads to unsmooth flow of electrons from one semiconductor to the other through the metal mediators. Thus, it remains a great challenge to construct a heterojunction in which the electron mediator is intimately contacted with two semiconductors with a large contact interface to achieve a fast photogenerated charge transfer.

Molybdenum carbide (Mo2C), as a typical kind of transition-metal carbide, has gained increasing attention due to its excellent electrical conductivity because the electronic structure of Mo2C is virtually analogous to that of Pt-group elements [44,45]. Mo2C has been extensively studied as a substitute for noble metals in various applications such as in chemical and electrochemical reactions [46–48]. More recently, Mo2C found important application in photocatalytic H2 evolution reactions [49–52]. For instance, Yi et al. designed CdS@Mo2C-C nanorods for photocatalytic H2 evolution, revealing that the Mo2C-C shell could protect the inner CdS from photocorrosion to eventually achieve high photostability [49]. Yue et al. synthesized SrTiO3@Mo2C core-shell nanostructure for highly enhanced photocatalytic H2 generation activity, and demonstrated that Mo2C was a highly active cocatalyst to dramatically boost the charge separation [51]. Therefore, it is envisioned that Mo2C can be coupled with g-C3N4 to obtain high photocatalytic activity. Moreover, it is worth noting that Mo2C is easily supported on a series of carbon materials including graphene [53], carbon fibers [54], and carbon sheets [55] resulting in a large surface area and high conductivity. In addition, the intimate conjugation between the anchored Mo2C particles and carbon supports can inhibit the aggregation of Mo2C, bringing about more active sites on the surface. Thus, it is expected that the carbon support of Mo2C could be utilized as an efficient electron mediator between Mo2C and g-C3N4 to fabricate a heterojunction with a fully contacted interface.

Here, for the first time, we design a novel non-noble-metal Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 heterostructure for highly efficient and stable visible-light-driven photocatalytic water splitting. The Mo2C@C was initially synthesized via a facile procedure, in which Mo2C nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots are well embedded in the carbon nanosheets. The Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 heterostructure was then obtained by depositing Mo2C@C onto the ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets. The carbon nanosheets at the interface between Mo2C and g-C3N4 serve as an extraordinary electron mediator to facilitate the spatial charge separation and migration between the two semiconductors and thus enhance the photocatalytic activity. Benefiting from the narrow band gap of Mo2C and the up-conversion property of the carbon quantum dots embedded in carbon nanosheets, the visible-light absorption of the photocatalysts was largely enhanced, thus endowing the heterostructure extraordinary water splitting capacity in the visible-light region. As a result, the 1% Mo2C@C/g-C3N4 composite displayed a remarkable hydrogen evolution activity without the additive Pt co-catalyst (52.1 μmol h−1), far exceeding that of the pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets (0.2 μmol h−1). This work provides a strategy for the design of non-noble-metal heterostructure with high spatial charge transfer efficiency.

5 g of dicyandiamide (DCDA) was placed in a covered ceramic crucible and then calcinated at 550 °C in air for 4 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the obtained bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) was ground into powder.
0.8 g of BCN was calcinated at 520 °C for 4 h in the air with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the g-C3N4 NSs (CN) were obtained.

Mo2C@C was fabricated via a hydrothermal process followed by thermal treatment. 0.5 g of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O together with 0.5 g of glucose were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was sealed and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation, thoroughly washed by deionized water and dried in an oven. Finally, the solid powder was calcinated at 850 °C for 3 h in 10% H2/Ar at a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1 to obtain Mo2C@C. To synthesize Mo2C, 0.4 g of glucose was used as the carbon source in the hydrothermal procedure.

A certain amount of Mo2C@C and 100 mg of CN was dispersed in 20 mL aqueous solution containing 50% ethanol. The dispersion was ultrasonicated for 30 min and then stirred for 12 h. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation, thoroughly washed by deionized water and then dried in an oven. The obtained powder was calcinated at 300 °C for 1 h in Ar, resulting in Mo2C@C/CN samples with 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2 wt% and 5 wt% Mo2C@C, respectively. Mo2C/CN sample with 1 wt% Mo2C was prepared with the same method of Mo2C@C/CN, with Mo2C@C replaced by Mo2C. The preparation process of Mo2C@C/CN is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The CQD were prepared with a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 1.5 g of glucose was added to 30 mL of deionized water to form a transparent solution. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove large insoluble particles, and then NaOH was added into the aqueous solution to adjust the pH to 7. The CQD solution was further purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a 3500 D membrane for 24 h. The obtained CQD aqueous solution was dried at 80 °C for 12 h to produce the CQD powder. Finally, the CQD powder was re-dispersed in deionized water to form a CQD aqueous solution.

X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained from a Bruker D2 Phaser using Cu/Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded by FEI Quanta 250 equipment. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM2100 TEM. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a TGA/DSC-1 system (Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland) under oxygen (60 mL/min) and Ar (20 mL/min) flow at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were conducted on a Quantachrome at liquid N2 temperature. The specific area was determined from the linear portion of the BET plot. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer with aluminum Kα radiation. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy was recorded by a V-670 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and transient PL decay spectra were both measured on a FLS980 spectrometer at room temperature using a 350 nm excitation wavelength.

The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out in a top-irradiation quartz cell connected to a closed glass gas circulation system (CEL-SPH2N, Beijing). In a photocatalytic reaction, 80 mg of sample was dispersed in 100 mL of aqueous solution containing 10% triethanolamine as sacrificial agent. The suspension was thoroughly evacuated and then irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter. The temperature of the reaction solution was kept at 6 °C during the photocatalytic reaction. The H2 evolved was analyzed with an on-line TCD gas chromatograph (GC-9720, nitrogen carrier).

The photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and flat-band potentials (Efb) measurements were carried out by an electrochemical station (CHI 660E) in a standard three-electrode quartz cell. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 5 mg of sample was suspended in 0.5 mL of deionized water containing 0.05 mL of 5 wt% Nafion D-520 dispersion, and the mixture was then dispersed by ultrasonication and spread onto an FTO glass. After being dried naturally, the FTO glass was heated at 120 °C for 1 h to evaporate the solvent. The prepared thin film was employed as working electrode, using a platinum plate as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. A 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm filter was used as visible light source for photocurrent measurement. 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte for the photocurrent and EIS measurements. 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte for the Efb measurement. The Efb measurement of samples was carried out on a frequency of 10 kHz.

The morphology of the samples was investigated by TEM and HRTEM. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, a large number of Mo2C nanoparticles are well integrated with the carbon nanosheets. Fig. 1B reveals that these nanoparticles have sizes in the range of 10–20 nm. Moreover, many carbon quantum dots (CQD) with a diameter of 3–5 nm are found to coexist with Mo2C in the carbon matrix. The formation of CQD could be attributed to the carbonization of glucose via hydrothermal treatment [56]. Pure CQD has been synthesized and its TEM image is shown in Fig. S1, revealing that the diameter of the particle is approximately 2–3 nm. HRTEM image in Fig. 1C further confirms the presence of Mo2C and CQD. As shown in Fig. 1C1 and C2, two kinds of clear interplanar distances are observed and measured to be 0.245 and 0.230 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) crystal planes of graphitic carbon and the (1 0 1) crystal planes of Mo2C, respectively [57]. Fig. S2 presents the TEM image of Mo2C, displaying that the Mo2C nanoparticles accumulate without being coated by carbon nanosheets. The inset HRTEM image exhibits crystal lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.230 nm assigned to the (1 0 1) crystal planes Mo2C. Fig. S3 displays the XRD patterns of Mo2C and Mo2C@C. The characterized diffraction peaks assigned to hexagonal Mo2C (β-Mo2C) (JCPDS NO. 35-0787) were clearly observed both for the Mo2C and Mo2C@C samples. In addition, both the samples exhibit an additional diffraction peak locating at 2θ = 36.9° corresponding to MoC (JCPDS NO. 65-3558), which can be seen as an intermediate product of β-Mo2C. Moreover, for Mo2C@C, a broad peak centered at 2θ = 25° reveals the formation of carbon, which is in consistence with the results of TEM characterization [58]. All the above observations indicate that excessive carbon source in the synthesis procedure results in a nanostructure where Mo2C nanoparticles and CQD are embedded in carbon nanosheets, while less amount of carbon source only produced Mo2C without carbon coating. To obtain the actual carbon content in Mo2C@C, TGA experiment was carried out in O2/Ar. In the oxidation process, the carbon was oxidized into CO2 or CO, and the Mo2C in Mo2C@C was converted completely to MoO3. As shown in Fig. S4, the first weight loss occurring between 50 and 200 °C was caused by the elimination of adsorbed water in the catalyst. This was followed by a slight increase in weight from 250 to 330 °C, indicating the oxidation of Mo2C to MoO3. The mass loss of approximately 9.9% from 330 to 450 °C was attributed to the oxidation of carbon to CO2 or CO. A steady decline in weight was observed above 700 °C, which could result from the sublimation of MoO3. As previously reported, MoO3 showed significant sublimation at temperatures above 700 °C [59–61]. Based on the above observation, the carbon content in the Mo2C@C was determined to be approximately 9.9 wt%.

To prepare g-C3N4 for a larger specific area and more active edges, the bulk g-C3N4 has been exfoliated into ultrathin g-C3N4 NSs prior to loading Mo2C@C. As shown in Fig. 2 , the exfoliated g-C3N4 presents ultrathin nanosheet structure with numerous micropores (Fig. 2B), in contrast to the stacked structure of bulk g-C3N4 (Fig. 2A). As seen from Fig. 2C and D, the Mo2C@C nanoparticles are well decorated on the surface of CN, manifesting the successful self-assembly of Mo2C@C and CN by electrostatic interactions. Fig. 2E clearly demonstrates the intimate heterojunction interfaces between CN and the Mo2C nanoparticles coupled with CQD bridged by the carbon nanosheets. Additionally, the HRTEM images in Fig. 2E1 and E2 exhibit lattice fringes corresponding to the interplanar distance of 0.230 and 0.245 nm, which can be ascribed to the (1 0 1) crystal planes of Mo2C and the (1 0 0) crystal planes of graphitic carbon [62], respectively. Furthermore, EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. 2F–J) reveal the spatial distribution of rich elements including carbon (C), nitrogen (N), molybdenum (Mo) and oxygen (O), further indicating that both Mo2C@C and CN phases are indeed fully mixed and intimately contacted, rather than existing as separate aggregates in the composites. For comparison, the morphology of Mo2C/CN was also analyzed by TEM and HRTEM characterizations. As displayed in Fig. S5, Mo2C agglomerates were successfully deposited on the surface of g-C3N4 NSs, while no apparent carbon nanosheets that coated on Mo2C nanoparticles were observed. Besides, XRD was carried out to investigate the phase structure of the prepared samples, and the results are presented in Fig. S6. For CN, the peak observed at 13.0° corresponds to the in-plane structural packing motif of tristriazine units and is indexed as the (1 0 0) plane of g-C3N4 [63]. The other peak centered at 27.4° is related to the interplanar stacking of conjugated aromatic rings, and can be assigned to the (0 0 2) plane of g-C3N4 [64]. All the Mo2C@C/CN samples only exhibit diffraction peaks corresponding to g-C3N4, which is due to the low content and high dispersion of Mo2C@C in the composites. Moreover, the characteristic diffraction peaks related to g-C3N4 in the Mo2C@C/CN samples display no apparent shift compared with those in the pristine g-C3N4, indicating that the coupling of Mo2C@C has no considerable effect on the phase structure of g-C3N4.

XPS was further carried out to investigate the chemical composition and elemental valence state of these as-prepared samples. XPS survey spectrum of 1% Mo2C@C/CN (Fig. 3 A) confirms that the composite consists of four elements including Mo, C, N and O. The observation of O element can be assigned to the formation of O-containing species on the surface of CN and the metal oxides on the surface of Mo2C. Fig. 3B depicts the Mo 3d XPS spectra of Mo2C@C and Mo2C@C/CN. For Mo2C@C, the peaks at 231.9 and 228.7 eV with a spin energy separation of 3.2 eV are assigned to Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 of Mo2+, revealing the formation of Mo2C [65]. Additional peaks correlated with Mo4+ in MoO2 and Mo6+ in MoO3 are also observed, which can be reasonably explained by the fact that the surfacial Mo2C can be partially oxidized to MoO2 and MoO3 when it is exposed to air [55,66]. The Mo 3d XPS spectrum of Mo2C@C/CN also confirms the existence of Mo2+, Mo4+ and Mo6+, respectively. Furthermore, it can be clearly observed that the Mo 3d XPS spectrum of Mo2C@C/CN hybrid shifts towards to lower band energies compared with that of Mo2C@C, which can be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between Mo2C@C and CN [67,68]. In Fig. 3C, the C1s XPS spectrum of Mo2C@C presents four peaks with binding energies at 288.9, 285.6, 284.8 and 284.1 eV. The peak observed at 284.1 eV is caused by the carbidic carbon in Mo2C [69]. The three peaks at 288.9, 285.6 and 284.8 eV can be attributed to CO, CO, and CC bonds, respectively [70,71]. Fig. 3D shows the O 1s XPS spectrum of Mo2C@C, presenting two peaks centered at 530.7 and 529.6 eV. The peak at 529.6 eV is associated with the O2− ions in the orthorhombic MoO3, while the peak at 530.7 eV corresponds to the O2− ions in the oxygen deficient MoO3−x [72,73]. This result is in consistence with the Mo4+ and Mo6+ states in Mo2C@C due to the partial oxidation of surfacial Mo2C. Fig. 3E displays the C 1s XPS spectra of CN and Mo2C@C/CN hybrid. The spectrum of C 1s of CN can be deconvoluted into four peaks. The peak at 288.9 eV corresponds to the CO bonds owing to some oxide species in CN. The two peaks at 288.3 and 285.6 eV are respectively attributed to the NCN and CN bonds in the s-triazine rings [30]. The peak at 288.4 eV can be assigned to the CC bonds deriving from the graphitic carbon in the carbon nitride matrix. Mo2C@C/CN shows similar C 1s XPS spectrum with CN due to the low content of Mo2C@C in the composite. The N 1s XPS spectra of CN and Mo2C@C/CN are shown in Fig. 3F, which both exhibit three peaks with binding energies at 400.9 eV, 399.7 eV and 399.1 eV. The dominant peak at 398.8 eV corresponds to the sp2-bonded N (CNC), and the peak located at 399.7 eV is usually attributed to the tertiary nitrogen N–(C)3 groups [74]. Besides, the peak at 400.8 eV is assigned to the amino groups (C–N–H) [75].

To determine the surface area of the as-synthesized samples, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were measured over the samples. Fig. S7A and B show the isotherm curves of CN and Mo2C@C/CN samples, together with the corresponding pore-size distribution. The specific surface area (SBET) of the samples are displayed in Table S1. All the Mo2C@C/CN samples exhibit smaller SBET and total pore volume (Vpore) than those of the CN sample, which can be ascribed to the fact the Mo2C@C covering on the surface of CN could reduce the number of exposed in-plane nanopores of CN.

To investigate the optical properties of the as-obtained samples, UV–vis absorption spectra were performed. As shown in Fig. 4 , CN exhibits poor visible-light absorption ability with an absorption edge at ∼450 nm. Both Mo2C and Mo2C@C demonstrate strong absorption in the wavelength range of 350–800 nm, which is consistent with their black color property. The light absorption for Mo2C@C is higher than that of Mo2C, probably owing to the excellent light-harvesting ability of the carbon shell of Mo2C. Mo2C@C/CN samples all display considerably enhanced optical absorption ability compared with CN, and the composites show stronger optical absorption with the increasing Mo2C@C content, indicating that the coupling of Mo2C@C could effectively improve the visible-light harvesting ability of the photocatalysts. In addition, it can be observed that Mo2C/CN exhibits an absorption capacity slightly lower than that of Mo2C@C/CN, demonstrating the photosensitization role of carbon nanosheets in Mo2C@C/CN hybrid.

Photocatalytic H2 evolution for different photocatalysts has been carried out in an aqueous solution containing TEOA (sacrificial electron donor) without the additive Pt co-catalyst under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). As seen from Fig. 5 A, CN displays negligible hydrogen-evolution rate (0.2 μmol h−1) under visible light owing to its limited light-harvesting efficiency and fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. No hydrogen evolution was detected for Mo2C@C. Interestingly, when Mo2C@C was loaded on CN, extraordinary photocatalytic H2 evolution rates were achieved over the Mo2C@C/CN heterostructures. By varying the ratio of Mo2C@C to CN, an optimum H2 evolution rate of 52.1 μmol h−1 was obtained for 1% Mo2C@C/CN hybrid, which was up to nearly 260 times that of CN. This remarkable enhancement in photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the intimately contacted interfaces and highly efficient charge transfer pathway built by carbon nanosheets between Mo2C and CN, which effectively accelerated the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes and thus inhibits their recombination. However, further increasing the amount of Mo2C@C would result in a decreased catalytic activity, indicating that appropriate ratio of Mo2C@C to CN is of vital importance to the efficient photocatalytic reaction, because excessive Mo2C@C may reduce the active sites for hydrogen production and also act as recombination centers of photoinduced charge carriers [51]. In contrast, 1% Mo2C/CN exhibits a H2 evolution rate of 36.9 μmol h−1, which is inferior to that of 1% Mo2C@C/CN, powerfully demonstrating the significant role of carbon nanosheets played in the enhancement of activity. The stability and recyclability for the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution over 1% Mo2C@C/CN have been investigated by four consecutive operations under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 5B, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of the sample decreased to 46.2 μmol h−1 after four successive experimental runs (∼16 h), retaining almost 89% of its original activity (52.1 μmol h−1) under visible-light irradiation. These results indicate that Mo2C@C/CN can serve as a promising photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution from water, with high activity and excellent recyclability. To further verify the stability and reusability of catalyst, the comparison in structure between the fresh and the recycled 1% Mo2C@C/CN sample has been carried out by XRD characterization method. The XRD patterns (Fig. S8) show that no apparent change was found for the recycled samples, verifying that the structure of catalysts could maintain after the photocatalytic reaction. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) over 1% Mo2C@C/CN was measured with different band-pass filters (band-pass filters at 400, 450, 500, 550 and 600 nm, ±10 nm). As displayed in Fig. S9, the action spectrum of the catalyst exhibited wavelength-dependent AQE values, with the highest value of 1.44% obtained at 400 nm (detailed calculation process of AQE is provided in Supporting Information). This AQE value is comparable to that of the previous reported Ni2P/g-C3N4 (1.8% at 420 nm) [76], CoO/g-C3N4 (1.91% at 420 nm) [77], g-C3N4-Pd-Cu2O (0.9% at 420 nm) [78], N-TiO2/g-C3N4 (1.2% at 420 nm) [79] and carbon/g-C3N4 (1.4% at 420 nm) [80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism in the Mo2C@C/CN hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: For better understanding the electron-hole transport mechanism, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient fluorescence decay spectra have been tested for CN, Mo2C@C/CN and Mo2C/CN samples. As shown in Fig. 6 A, upon the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, all the photocatalysts show a broad PL emission peak centered at around 460 nm, which corresponds to the band-band PL due to the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers of g-C3N4. An extremely intense peak can be easily observed in the PL spectrum of CN, suggesting a fairly high photoinduced charge recombination rate. Notably, the decorating of Mo2C@C on CN results in considerable PL quenching, indicating the greatly suppressed recombination of electron-hole pairs in Mo2C@C/CN heterostructure. Besides, it can be found that the PL intensity reaches minimum for 1% Mo2C@C/CN and then increases with the higher ratio of Mo2C@C to CN. This result reveals that excessive Mo2C@C may become recombination centers of photoinduced charge carriers and impede charge separation efficiency. Moreover, as a contrast to 1% Mo2C@C/CN, 1% Mo2C/CN exhibits a higher PL emission intensity, indicative of the advantages of ternary heterojunction, which manifests that the carbon nanosheets reacted as a remarkable electron mediator leading to the efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs between Mo2C and CN. The above results can be further evidenced by transient photocurrent response measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). It is clearly seen from Fig. 6B that 1% Mo2C@C/CN shows much enhanced photocurrent response than 1% Mo2C/CN and CN, and also exhibits the smallest radius of Nyquist circle in Fig. 6C. The observations strongly demonstrate that 1% Mo2C@C/CN has the lowest charge transfer resistance and the most efficient interfacial charge migration process.

To gain deeper insights into the interfacial charge transfer kinetics in photocatalytic reaction, the transient fluorescence decay spectra for CN, 1% Mo2C/CN and 1% Mo2C@C/CN have been tested (Fig. 7 ), and the detailed kinetic parameters for the photocatalysts are listed in the inset table in Fig. 7. A tri-exponential function fitting was employed to analyze the PL decay curves, and the average lifetime <τ> was calculated using Eq. (1). It is found that the coupling of Mo2C with CN reduces the lifetime from 8.72 ns (CN) to 8.16 ns (1% Mo2C/CN), while the incorporating of carbon nanosheets onto Mo2C further reduces the lifetime to 8.02 ns (1% Mo2C@C/CN). The decrease of lifetime strongly suggests the significantly accelerated photo-exciton dissociation in the photocatalysts, confirming the spatially separated photocarriers at the interface between the semiconductors [81,82]. This result can be primarily attributed to the formation of the heterojunction between Mo2C and CN, which considerably accelerates the dynamics process of photoinduced charge separation and transfer. Moreover, the carbon nanosheets with extraordinary conductivity could perform as electron mediator that effectively strengthens the interfacial transfer of charge pairs between Mo2C and CN, thus dramatically reduces the chance of their recombination. Lastly, the CQD embedded in carbon nanosheets could also provide high photocarrier transfer forces to drive electron-hole pair spatial separation [83]. On the basis of PL, transient photocurrent responses, EIS as well as transient fluorescence decay spectra, it can be well revealed that an efficient spatial separation of photo-generated charge carriers have been achieved in the Mo2C@C/CN heterostructure, and thus contributing to the highly efficient visible-light-driven H2 evolution from water reduction.
(1)
< τ > =
τ
1
α
1
+
τ
2
α
2
+
τ
3
α
3

Scheme 2 A and B illustrate the charge transfer processes over Mo2C/CN and Mo2C@C/CN heterostructures, respectively. For Mo2C/CN, the Mo2C nanoparticles are in contact with CN in a point-to-face way, and only a small quantity of charge transfer pathways is generated on the interfaces, resulting in a slow charge transfer process and a limited photocatalytic activity. However, in Mo2C@C/CN heterostructure, the carbon nanosheets coated on the surface of Mo2C are in face-to-face contact with CN, which constructs numerous fast charge transfer channels between the two semiconductors, strongly accelerating the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers. The comparison between the two composites clearly highlights the importance of the carbon nanosheets performing as an electron mediator and demonstrates the outstanding charge transfer ability of the well-designed Mo2C@C/CN heterostructure.

Based on the above discussion, a postulated photocatalytic mechanism for the Mo2C@C/CN hybrid has been proposed and depicted in Scheme 2C. According to the analysis on the band structures of Mo2C and CN, the CB and VB positions of Mo2C stand between those of CN, thus a type-I heterojunction with carbon nanosheets as an ideal mediator can be formed [85]. Although type-I heterojunction is theoretically disadvantageous for charge carrier separation, many systems of such heterojunction has been reported, such as Mo2C/TiO2 [50], Mo2C/SrTiO3 [51], ZnIn2S4/g-C3N4 and WO3/TiO2 [86,87]. For Mo2C@C/CN heterostructure, upon visible-light irradiation, both of Mo2C and CN could be excited to produce considerable photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The ultrathin CN NSs allow charge carriers to migrate from the inner part to the surface with a shorter distance, while the in-plane micropores endow CN with sufficient diffusion channels that strongly speed up the charge transfer. Subsequently, the photoinduced electrons on the CB of CN would easily transfer to the CB of Mo2C for H2 generation through the Mo2C/carbon/CN interfaces, where the CB potential difference between Mo2C and CN provides great driving force for the migration of photogenerated electrons, and the carbon nanosheets with excellent conductivity greatly accelerate the interfacial charge transfer. Meanwhile, the photoinduced holes on the VB of CN can be quickly captured by the sacrificial agent (TEOA) or migrate to the VB of Mo2C via the carbon mediator to be consumed by TEOA, leading to the efficient spatial separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. Typically, the CQD can also efficiently absorb longer-wavelength near-infrared light and transform it into shorter-wavelength light by its characteristic up-conversion photoluminescence property (Fig. S11), which is then absorbed by CN to produce additional electron-hole pairs [86]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the superior photocatalytic performance of Mo2C@C/CN heterostructure can be mainly attributed to three reasons: (1) The matchable band alignment of Mo2C and CN provides strong driving forces for the migration and separation of photogenerated electrons in the heterostructure; (2) The carbon nanosheets act as a binder to bring great affinity to the Mo2C/CN interface and establish plentiful interfacial electron transfer channels between Mo2C and CN, which significantly accelerates the charge transfer and suppresses the charge recombination; (3) The up-conversion property of CQD could strengthen the visible-light absorption of the photocatalyst. All these positive factors simultaneously contribute to the extraordinary visible-light-driven photocatalytic performance of the Mo2C@C/CN composites.

In summary, a novel noble-metal-free Mo2C@C/CN photocatalyst was rationally designed for the first time. A remarkable activity in visible-light-driven hydrogen evolution has been achieved, with a hydrogen evolution rate of 52.1 μmol h−1 for 1% Mo2C@C/CN hybrid, which was nearly 260 times that of CN. The suitable band matching between Mo2C and CN allows kinetics-driven spatial charge separation and transfer, while the intercalated carbon nanosheets serve as an extraordinary electron mediator to greatly facilitate the photo-induced charge mobility. Besides, the extended visible light absorption range provided by the up-conversion photoluminescence character of CQD is also a contributing factor for the remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity. This work provides a strategy for designing heterojunctions with highly efficient interfacial charge transfer for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (xjj2018192), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, China (KF1710), the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces, Xiamen University, China (201715), the State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Xi’an Jiaotong University, China (EIPE18311). We thank Miss Jiamei Liu and Miss Jiao Li at Instrument Analysis Center of Xi’an Jiaotong University for their assistance with XPS and TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.12.102.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
